data.title: Clueless AOC stumbles at Munich: Confused about Venezuela, cowboys
data.description:
I made fun of Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (D-N.Y.) yesterday on Rising after listening to her get tripped up when discussing U.S. Taiwan policy at the Munich Security Conference, but her remarks on that topic were brilliant and statesmanlike compared to what she had to say about South America! Can AOC even find the continent on a map? After listening to her say this, I’m doubtful. 

Quick fact-check there: The country of Venezuela is entirely north of the equator. You can consult a map if you don’t believe me. Perhaps AOC should put as much effort into learning geography as she does perfecting her Latin-American accent. I’m envious of her rolled-Rs, to be sure, but I’d like a possible presidential contender to know even basic facts about the areas of the world she’s discussing. 

But honestly, the Venezuela gaffe was not as significant as this next one — more troubling than a mere geography blunder, AOC doesn’t appear to understand the history of the Americas, and the role that European colonization played in it. Watch this misinformed criticism she made of Secretary of State Marco Rubio’s remarks on the origins of cowboy culture. 

Uh, what? Spain is absolutely the originator of cowboy culture, not enslaved African persons. It was the Europeans who re-introduced horses to the Americas, after they had died out thousands of years before. In South America, that was the Spanish conquistadors. The vaqueros — Mexican cowboys — had distinct Spanish influences, and that influence spread up through Texas to the American southwest.  

It’s disconcerting that AOC would go to Europe and then minimize the achievements and contributions of Europeans to American culture. I’m also troubled that the Europeans in the room laughed along with her. Do they want their own culture erased? Is this what progressive leftism — the ideology of AOC — seeks to achieve? 

Yet I was assured by liberal Democrats that it’s President Trump and Vice President Vance who are the ones offending Europeans all the time and damaging the U.S.-Europe relationship by downplaying the significance of it. AOC is downplaying our shared history, in service of uplifting a false narrative about native and marginalized peoples, I suppose, which is ultimately the end of game of cultural leftism: historical revisionism in the service of identity politics. 

Thank goodness we had Rubio there, setting everybody straight. Maybe he could offer to tutor AOC in Latin-American history and geography. I hear he doesn’t have quite enough jobs to do yet. 

Robby Soave is co-host of The Hill’s commentary show “Rising” and a senior editor for Reason Magazine. This column is an edited transcription of his daily commentary.

data.title: Democrats' investments in Black and Latino voters will pay off
data.description:
As the 2026 midterms approach, a question looms for Democratic donors and organizers: Should they invest in Black and Latino voter registration and engagement despite declining support among these groups in 2024?

The concern reflects real data. Comparing the 2024 American Electorate Voter Poll to the 2020 American Election Eve Poll — both of which my firm, the African American Research Collaborative, co-led — Democratic margins dropped by 23 points among Latino men and by 16 points among Black men.

The skepticism is understandable. Here’s why it’s wrong.

First, there’s the principle. Race, ethnicity and income should not determine who gets to vote in America. The registration gap between white voters and voters of color undermines democracy regardless of partisan outcomes. Lake Research found that 30 percent of eligible Black citizens and 39 percent of eligible Latino citizens remain unregistered. These gaps persist because Black and Latino Americans, younger and less wealthy, move more frequently, requiring re-registration.

The base is also a factor. Black and Latino support for Democrats is still essential. In presidential elections, the Democrats haven’t won a majority of white voters since 1964. Every victory since then has required strong turnout from communities of color. Catalist found this turnout fell in 2024, dropping 6 points among Black voters and by 3 points among Latinos. Support for Democrats also narrowed, but Black voters backed Democrats by 70 points and Latinos by 24 points.

Research tells us these voters want affordability. In November 2024, the cost of living dominated. We tested policies addressing it: down payment assistance, price gouging bans and lower pharmaceutical costs. Each policy polled 6 to 14 points better than Democratic House candidates among Black voters and 18 to 32 points better among Latinos.

Scott Holiday came up in the labor movement and now runs Detroit Action, a grassroots economic justice organization. When organizing, he told me his team hears: “Housing and utilities are the big two. People paying their rent can’t afford their utilities or vice versa. Folks are desperate for some relief.”

Detroit is not unique. In November, Democrats won in Virginia, New Jersey and New York City on the issue of affordability. Abigail Spanberger (D) won Virginia’s governorship by 15 points running on housing, utilities, and prescription drugs — winning economy voters by 20 points in ABC News exit polls.

There is also a two-prong battle where Republicans suppress Black and Latino voting power, then persuade those who remain to join them. Florida’s Republican legislation imposes fines up to $250,000 on voter registration organizations and makes compliance nearly impossible. Florida’s League of Women Voters now won’t even collect paper registrations. 

Who loses? University of Florida research finds Black and Latino voters registered via these organizations five times more often than white voters.

Due to Texas’s mid-decade redistricting gerrymander, the Lawyers’ Committee finds white voters now control 70 percent of Texas congressional districts, despite being 40 percent of the population.

Simultaneously, conservatives work to win voters of color. Groups like LIBRE Initiative, a Koch-funded 13 state network, conduct year-round Latino voter education. Emily Persaud-Zamora leads Silver State Voices, Nevada’s civic engagement hub.

“I live in East Las Vegas, the historic Latino part of town since the mid-1980s,” she told me. “In 2024, I saw LIBRE and other Republican entities canvassing my neighborhood before Democrats. They’ve done the work to see that it’s not just about ‘vote for this or vote for that.’ They meet people where their needs are, and that’s how they’re getting folks wrapped in.”

We need aggressive registration to rebuild what suppression has taken, and sustained engagement to win registered voters.

The mechanics of registration also complicate things. Registration builds voter lists. These enable everything else: door knocking, direct mail, digital outreach, phone banks. More Black and Latino citizens on the rolls means more to speak with and turn out.

Yet, in 2024, 42 percent of Black and 45 percent of Latino voters told us they weren’t contacted about their vote. That’s an infrastructure and strategy failure. Holiday sees it in Detroit: “When we heard ‘both parties are the same’ or ‘Democrats only come around at election time,’ these aren’t people ignored for one cycle.”

Voter contact makes lists useful for listening to voters, demonstrating responsiveness and sharing solutions. Not only new or infrequent voters need contact. We’ve found 80 percent of Black voters over 60 encourage friends or family to vote. Engagement should turn committed voters into educators on registration, voting and candidates.

Finally, there must be a realistic investment timeline. Registration and engagement require time to build operations and trust. As Holiday told me, “Earlier investment allows us to build deeper and warmer lists. It creates opportunities to bring people passionate about issues into the organization to begin working in their community.”

Deprioritize early registration for late turnout, and you face a smaller voter universe with no foundation to build on. Register without engaging, and you cede the conversation to the other side.

The math hasn’t changed — Black and Latino voters still matter. The question is how to reach them. Lead with affordability. Register to counter suppression. Engage year-round. Turn out en masse. Win, then deliver on promises.

Skip any stage, and the others become exponentially harder.

Henry Fernandez is CEO of the African American Research Collaborative, a quantitative and qualitative research firm. He has previously led grassroots, digital, legislative, and electoral campaigns from alderman to governor.

data.title: Person with gun arrested near Capitol
data.description:
The U.S. Capitol Police (USCP) said Tuesday afternoon that its officers arrested a person “with what appears to be a gun” in front of the Capitol.

“Our officers just arrested a person with what appears to be a gun near the West Front of the U.S. Capitol Building,” USCP wrote on social platform X at 12:34 p.m. EST. 

“Please avoid the area. We will provide more information when new information is confirmed,” they added.

Two minutes later, USCP wrote that Maryland Avenue is “temporarily closed” between First and Third Streets, SW, while it investigates. At 12:53 p.m. EST, the department wrote, “At this time, there does not appear to be any other suspects or ongoing threat.”

“Out of an abundance of caution, please continue to stay away from the area while we investigate and collect evidence,” USCP added. “Again, at this time, one person is in custody.”

An officer from the department confirmed to NewsNation that the suspect had a long gun on him. 

Congress is not in session this week, so lawmakers are not in the Capitol. Both chambers are set to return on Monday.

data.title: Utah governor blasts Trump regulator for blessing prediction markets
data.description:
Utah Gov. Spencer Cox (R) ripped a top Trump administration regulator Tuesday over its push to defeat state efforts to regulate prediction markets.

In a Tuesday post on social media, Cox criticized the Commodity Futures Trading Commission’s (CFTC) plans to fight against state laws and litigation meant to impose rules on prediction markets.

“These prediction markets you are breathlessly defending are gambling—pure and simple. They are destroying the lives of families and countless Americans, especially young men. They have no place in Utah,” Cox said.

CFTC Chair Mike Selig announced Tuesday the agency would challenge any state’s attempt to regulate prediction markets, a job he said belonged only to federal watchdogs.

“The CFTC is taking an important step to ensure that these markets have a place here in America and have the integrity and resilience and vibrancy that our derivatives markets deserve,” Selig said in a video posted on the social platform X.

“To those who seek to challenge our authority in this space, let me be clear: We will see you in court,” he continued.

Utah’s state constitution bans gambling of any kind, preventing residents from using one of the myriad online betting platforms that have skyrocketed in popularity. But prediction markets, which allow participants to bet on the outcome of certain events, have created a workaround for Utahns eager to gamble.

Sports books allow users to gamble on the binary outcomes of certain events. Prediction markets, however, offer “event contracts” tied to a much wider range of potential occurrences, similar to the the commodity futures contracts overseen by the CFTC.

Through event contracts, prediction markets allow users to bet on everything from sports to military operations in similar ways through which a Wall Street trader might short a company’s stock or the price of oil.

Defenders of prediction markets say such companies give users more power and flexibility over their own financial futures through a much wider range of investments. But critics say there is no meaningful difference between prediction markets and sports books in terms of the dangers they pose to broader society.

In his post responding to Selig, Cox mocked Selig for “attempting this with a straight face” and vowed to fight the CFTC’s efforts.

“Mike, I appreciate you attempting this with a straight face, but I don’t remember the CFTC having authority over the “derivative market” of LeBron James rebounds.

“Let me be clear, I will use every resource within my disposal as governor of the sovereign state of Utah, and under the Constitution of the United States to beat you in court.”

data.title: Springsteen rips 'wannabe king' Trump, launches concert tour 'in defense of America'
data.description:
Bruce Springsteen says he’s launching a nationwide concert tour “in defense of America,” adding the country’s ideals are “under attack” by President Trump and “his rogue government.”

“Fans, friends, and good folk from coast to coast: We are living through dark, disturbing and dangerous times, but do not despair, the cavalry is coming,” Springsteen said in a video message posted Tuesday on Instagram.

“Bruce Springsteen and the E Street Band will be taking the stage this spring from Minneapolis, to California, to Texas, to Washington, D.C., for the ‘Land of Hope and Dreams’ American tour,” the “Born in the USA” singer said.

“We will be rocking your town in celebration and in defense of America, American democracy, American freedom, our American Constitution and our sacred American dream,” Springsteen, 76, continued, “all of which are under attack by our wannabe king and his rogue government in Washington, D.C.”

“Everyone — regardless of where you stand or what you believe in — is welcome,” he said.

“So come out and join the United Free Republic of E Street Nation for an American spring of rock and rebellion.”

Springsteen, a frequent critic of Trump, released an anti-Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) song, “Streets of Minneapolis,” last month following the two high-profile shooting deaths involving federal law enforcement in the city. On Tuesday, Springsteen said his band’s concert tour would kick off in Minneapolis on March 31.

Last year, the rocker said that the U.S. was “currently in the hands of a corrupt, incompetent and treasonous administration.”

“There’s some very weird, strange and dangerous s‑‑‑ going on out there right now. In America, they are persecuting people for using their right to free speech and voicing their dissent,” he said at the time.

Trump responded in a social media post last May with an edited video that depicted him hitting Springsteen with an animated golf ball. 

The president has said of Springsteen, “Never liked him, never liked his music, or his Radical Left Politics and, importantly, he’s not a talented guy — Just a pushy, obnoxious JERK, who fervently supported Crooked Joe Biden, a mentally incompetent FOOL, and our WORST EVER President, who came close to destroying our Country.”

Springsteen’s tour is poised to head to 20 cities. The final concert is scheduled at Nationals Park in Washington on May 27.

data.title: Hillary Clinton on Epstein connection: 'We have no links'
data.description:
Former Secretary of State Hillary Clinton told the BBC that she and her husband, former President Clinton, have “no links” to Jeffrey Epstein and Ghislaine Maxwell.

“We have no links,” the former first lady said in an interview published Tuesday. “We have a very clear record that we’d been willing to talk about, which my husband has said he took some rides on the airplane for [Epstein’s] charitable work. I don’t recall ever meeting him.”

When BBC’s Jessica Parker asked Hillary Clinton whether she ever met Maxwell, she replied, “I did, on a few occasions.”

Bill Clinton is featured in files related to Epstein released by the Department of Justice. The former president’s name appeared in flight logs from 2002 and 2003, documenting his travel with Epstein to Thailand, Portugal, Ghana, Russia and China — before the financier pleaded guilty to procuring a child for prostitution and soliciting a prostitute. 

The Clintons have not been accused of wrongdoing in connection with Epstein or Maxwell. 

CNN also reported in August that Maxwell was an honored guest at a Clinton Global Initiative conference in 2013, after she was publicly accused in civil lawsuits of assisting Epstein to sexually abusing minors. 

But Hillary Clinton defended Maxwell’s presence at the conference, telling the BBC that “thousands of people go to the Clinton Global Initiative.” 

“So, to me, it’s not something that is really at the heart of what this matter is about,” she added of she and her husband’s connection to Epstein and Maxwell. “They are accused and, in both cases, were convicted of horrific crimes against girls and women. That should be the focus.”

The former first couple will testify separately in front of the House Oversight and Government Reform Committee next week, as part of the panel’s probe into the Epstein case. The Clintons initially resisted subpoenas from committee Chair James Comer (R-Ky.), before relenting earlier this month. 

The Clintons have called for their hearings to be held in public, with Hillary Clinton doubling down on that push to the BBC. 

“We will show up but we think it would be better to have it in public,” she said.

data.title: FCC commissioner condemns 'censorship' following Stephen Colbert comments on Talarico interview
data.description:
A Federal Communications Commission (FCC) member is going after Paramount over the company’s apparent decision to bar CBS late night host Stephen Colbert from airing an interview with Texas Democratic Senate candidate James Talarico.

“This is yet another troubling example of corporate capitulation in the face of this Administration’s broader campaign to censor and control speech,” said Commissioner Anna Gomez, a Democrat. “The FCC has no lawful authority to pressure broadcasters for political purposes or to create a climate that chills free expression.”

Gomez added, “CBS is fully protected under the First Amendment to determine what interviews it airs, which makes its decision to yield to political pressure all the more disappointing.”

Colbert made headlines overnight when he announced on his program Monday the network’s lawyers had told him “in no uncertain terms … that we could not have him on the broadcast.”

“Not only could I not have him on, I could not mention me not having him on,” the comedian continued. “And because my network clearly does not want us to talk about this, let’s talk about this.”

Colbert theorized that Paramount did not want to upset FCC Chair Brendan Carr, who recently floated doing away with an agency rule exempting talk shows from being required to provide equal time on air to candidates for political office.

“Let’s just call this what it is: Donald Trump’s administration wants to silence anyone who says anything bad about Trump on TV, because all Trump does is watch TV,” the “Late Show” host said.

Gomez said, “It is no secret that Paramount, CBS’s parent company, has regulatory matters before the government, but corporate interests cannot justify retreating from airing newsworthy content.”

“The FCC is powerless to impose restrictions on protected speech, and any attempt to intimidate broadcasters into self-censorship undermines both press freedom and public trust,” she added in her statement.

Carr has been a longtime critic of the mainstream media and has insisted major broadcasters could be subject to license scrutiny if they are not publishing content that serves what he has called “the public interest.”

Colbert’s show was canceled last year and is slated to come off the air this spring.

After Monday’s episode aired, Talarico posted on social media saying the episode is evidence that “Trump is worried we’re about to flip Texas.” 

Paramount did not respond to The Hill’s request for comment.

data.title: Greene: Loomer 'divisiveness,' 'manufactured bulls‑‑‑' have been 'great' for Trump
data.description:
Former Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene (R-Ga.) hit back at conservative influencer Laura Loomer on social media Tuesday after Loomer said she expected Greene to “do nothing but attack President Trump” through the midterms in November.

“I have to admit I was wrong when I warned Trump and his senior staff to stay away from Laura Loomer,” Greene quipped in a post on the social platform X, referencing when she advised Trump’s campaign against hiring Loomer.  

“Her divisiveness, loyalty tests temper tantrums, Israel First agenda, and her manufactured bulls— she calls loomered have all been great for the party and the President!” the Georgia Republican wrote.

Greene pushed back against Loomer, who suggested in a post on X that the former lawmaker “wants the GOP to lose the midterms” after Greene responded to comments made by President Trump aboard Air Force One on Monday.

Trump was asked about his upcoming trip to Rome, Ga., this week, where he is expected to deliver remarks on affordability and the economy. Rome is located in northwest Georgia and is part of the district that Greene once represented, before she stepped down earlier this year.

“Well, we have a lot of people that want to take Marjorie Traitor Greene’s place. Many, many candidates, and I have to choose one. And they say whoever I endorse is going to win,” the president told reporters.   

Greene called the epithet a “badge of honor” in a separate post.

“Him calling me that has become a badge of honor because I’m not in his cult, I refused to bow to his demands to cover up the Epstein files to protect his friends, and I stopped fighting the hardest for a team that intentionally refuses to win,” she wrote on X.

Greene and Loomer often take shots at each other on social media, hurling insults, name-calling, and debating one another’s MAGA bona fides.

Loomer, known for spreading conspiracy theories and making inflammatory remarks online, has become a close ally of Trump, while Greene’s once-close relationship with the president soured last year as the two drifted apart on a variety of issues.

While Trump has seemingly embraced Loomer at times, including saying last April that he “sometimes” listens to her policy and personnel recommendations, others in the White House have sought more distance.

After a meeting between Loomer and Vice President Vance last summer, officials maintained that Loomer was not serving as an advisor to the administration, formally or informally.  

The far-right activist has reportedly sought press credentials for the White House, which she has not received. She was credentialed to join the Pentagon press corps in November 2025 following Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth’s media policy changes.

Greene on Tuesday mocked Loomer’s seeming desire for greater influence within the party.

“PLEASE make her Chair of the RNC! Wait that is not enough,” she wrote. “Make her White House Chief of Staff!! How dare President Trump not reward his most loyal worshipper! Literally the self-appointed High Priestess of MAGA or MIGA or whatever this thing has mutated into.”

“I can’t believe they haven’t even given Laura the most simple and basic of all things, White House press credentials. This is an outrage!!” the former lawmaker added.

data.title: Trump's NATO ambassador knocks Ocasio-Cortez over Munich appearance
data.description:
U.S. Ambassador to NATO Matthew Whitaker on Tuesday knocked Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (D-N.Y.) over her appearance at the Munich Security Conference last week, saying she does not have a response to President Trump’s “very clear articulation” and international successes.

“It was interesting to be in the room and to have her and Gov. Whitmer in this very intimate discussion about these important issues, and I was really surprised that both of them didn’t have better answers or at least better preparation,” Whitaker said on Fox News’s “Fox & Friends” on Fox News, referring to Michigan Gov. Gretchen Whitmer (D).

“I think it’s really quite obvious that the left really does not have an answer for the president’s very clear articulation and success on the international stage, and they’re struggling to do that,” he later told co-host Charlie Hurt.

Whitaker pointed to Ocasio-Cortez’s response to whether the U.S. should commit troops to defending Taiwan if China were to invade, in which she stammered before responding. The ambassador said it is “so obvious” on how to respond to the issue in keeping stability and maintaining U.S. interests in the region.

“What we find when people try to elevate — when people think they look in the mirror and see themselves as a presidential candidate, you really have to be prepared for the world stage,” Whitaker said. “And Munich is the biggest world stage when it comes to these foreign policy issues.”

He said what Ocasio-Cortez and other Democrats had to say in Munich faltered compared to Secretary of State Marco Rubio’s “very strong message.” That speech, Whitaker noted, showed that the U.S. is “not interested in being polite, orderly caretakers of the west’s managed decline.”

Ocasio-Cortez’s presence at Munich drew mixed responses, from both Democrats and President Trump, who said her answering questions was “not a good look for the United States.”

The New York Democrat, seen as the political heir to Sen. Bernie Sanders (I-Vt.), received praise from European leaders over her youth and leftward politics.

“I’m not sure America’s ready for that yet,” former Irish President Mary Robinson told The New York Times. “But maybe it is.”

She later told the Times that she went to Munich “not because I’m running for president, not because I’ve made some kind of decision about a horse race or a candidacy, but because we need to sound the alarm bells that a lot of those folks in nicely pressed suits in that room will not be there much longer if we do not do something about the runaway inequality that is fueling far-right populist movements.”

data.title: Nancy Guthrie family cleared by investigators
data.description:
(NewsNation) — The Pima County Sheriff’s Office said Monday all members of Nancy Guthrie’s family, including their spouses, have been cleared as suspects.

“The family has been nothing but cooperative and gracious and are victims in this case,” the department said in a statement.

The office went on to ask the media to exercise professionalism and compassion when reporting on the case, saying that suggesting the family is anything other than victims was “cruel.”

The search for Guthrie has entered a third week, with investigators focusing efforts on DNA testing of a black glove found not far from her home. Investigators say the glove appears to match one worn by a suspect whose image was captured on surveillance video.

Video and photos released by the FBI show a masked person with a gun appearing to tamper with the camera at Guthrie’s front door, at one point using shrubbery to hide their face.

The FBI has described the man as standing 5 feet, 9 inches or 5 feet, 10 inches tall, with an average build.

Also on Monday, TMZ reported a fourth email from someone offering information on the kidnappers in exchange for Bitcoin. The outlet publicly offered to work with the writer to send information to the FBI and ensure they would get the offered reward.

The emails sent to TMZ are separate from an earlier ransom demand sent to the outlet. Two ransom deadlines have passed in the case, although investigators have not publicly confirmed whether the alleged kidnappers have communicated with the family.

In a new video posted Sunday night, Savannah Guthrie urged “whoever has” her mother to return her to her family.

“It’s been two weeks since our mom was taken and I just wanted to come on and say that, um, we still have hope. And we still believe,” Savannah Guthrie said in the video. “And I wanted to say to whoever has her, or knows where she is, that it’s never too late, and you’re not lost or alone. And it is never too late to do the right thing, and we are here.”

Authorities believe Guthrie was kidnapped in the late night or early morning hours before she was reported missing Feb. 1.

The FBI is offering a reward of up to $100,000 for information leading to Guthrie’s recovery or the arrest and conviction of anyone involved in her disappearance.

Anyone with “actionable information” is asked to contact 1-800-CALL-FBI or the Pima County Sheriff’s Department nonemergency line at 520-351-4900.

Nexstar’s Michael Bartiromo contributed to this report.
